umbrellas.label: Work by a Constant Force
umbrellas.description: How a constant force does work on an object, including the role of the angle between force and displacement.

intro.label: Work by a Constant Force Overview
intro.description: Work measures how much energy a force transfers to an object by acting over a displacement. The key insight is that only the component of force parallel to the displacement does work, captured by the dot product $W = Fd\cos\theta$. This umbrella covers how to compute and interpret work done by a single constant force, setting up the energy concepts that follow.
examples: force perpendicular to motion does zero work

in_depth.label: Work done by a constant force: $W = Fd\cos\theta$
in_depth.description: Calculate the work done by a constant force using $W = Fd\cos\theta$, where $F$ is the force magnitude, $d$ is the displacement magnitude, and $\theta$ is the angle between them; work is measured in joules (J = N·m). This focuses on the scalar dot-product definition for a single constant force, not work done by a varying force (which requires integration, beyond MCAT scope).

in_depth.label: Work done by gravity
in_depth.description: Apply $W = Fd\cos\theta$ to gravity: gravity does positive work when an object descends ($W = mgh$, with $h$ the drop) and negative work when it rises. This focuses on the gravitational force as the agent doing work, not the general conservation of energy (a separate umbrella).
examples: object rises: gravity does negative work
horizontal motion: gravity does zero work

in_depth.label: Work done by friction
in_depth.description: Recognize that kinetic friction does negative work on a sliding object because the friction force always opposes displacement ($\theta = 180°$, so $W_f = -f_k d$); static friction does zero work when there is no slipping. This focuses on friction as the agent, not the net-work concept (a separate keyword).
examples: friction converts KE to thermal energy

in_depth.label: Net work and superposition of forces
in_depth.description: Compute net work on an object as the algebraic sum of work done by each individual force ($W_{net} = \sum W_i$), or equivalently by applying $W = F_{net}d\cos\theta$. This focuses on summing contributions from multiple agents; the link between net work and kinetic energy change is the Work–Kinetic Energy Theorem (a separate keyword).
examples: zero net work → no change in speed

umbrellas.label: Mechanical Advantage & Simple Machines
umbrellas.description: How simple machines trade force for distance (or vice versa), allowing a smaller input force to do the same work.

intro.label: Mechanical Advantage Overview
intro.description: A simple machine multiplies the applied force at the cost of a longer displacement, so that the total work input equals the total work output (in an ideal machine). Mechanical advantage $MA = F_{out}/F_{in}$ quantifies this trade-off. This umbrella covers the concept and its application to levers, pulleys, and inclined planes.
examples: longer input distance → smaller input force needed
ideal machine: work in = work out

in_depth.label: Ideal mechanical advantage
in_depth.description: Define and calculate ideal mechanical advantage as $MA = F_{out}/F_{in} = d_{in}/d_{out}$: a machine that multiplies force by $MA$ requires the input to move $MA$ times farther than the output, so net work is conserved. This focuses on the ideal (frictionless) case; real machines have efficiency less than 100% (a natural extension not separately itemized at this level).
examples: lever with long input arm: large $MA$

in_depth.label: Levers and mechanical advantage
in_depth.description: Apply the lever principle — $F_1 \cdot d_1 = F_2 \cdot d_2$ about the fulcrum — to determine mechanical advantage of first-, second-, and third-class levers, identifying which arm length is input vs. output. This focuses on levers; pulley systems are a separate keyword.
examples: seesaw (class 1): fulcrum between effort and load
wheelbarrow (class 2): load between fulcrum and effort

in_depth.label: Pulleys and inclined planes: mechanical advantage
in_depth.description: Determine the mechanical advantage of a pulley system (number of supporting rope segments) and of an inclined plane ($MA = L/h$, where $L$ is ramp length and $h$ is height), recognizing that both reduce required force by increasing input distance. This focuses on these two simple machine types, not levers (a separate keyword).

umbrellas.label: Work–Kinetic Energy Theorem
umbrellas.description: The fundamental link between net work done on an object and the resulting change in its kinetic energy.

intro.label: Work–Kinetic Energy Theorem Overview
intro.description: The Work–Kinetic Energy Theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy: $W_{net} = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv_f^2 - \frac{1}{2}mv_i^2$. This is a powerful scalar relationship that connects force, displacement, mass, and velocity without needing to track time or acceleration directly.
examples: find final speed after known net work

in_depth.label: Applying the Work–Kinetic Energy Theorem
in_depth.description: Use $W_{net} = \frac{1}{2}mv_f^2 - \frac{1}{2}mv_i^2$ to find final speed, stopping distance, or net force when direct kinematics would require more steps; recognize that zero net work means constant kinetic energy. This focuses on relating net work to KE change for translation; rotational kinetic energy is beyond MCAT scope.
examples: braking car: friction's net work = $-\Delta KE$

umbrellas.label: Conservative vs. Non-Conservative Forces
umbrellas.description: The distinction between forces whose work is path-independent (conservative) and those that dissipate energy (non-conservative).

intro.label: Conservative Forces Overview
intro.description: A conservative force does work that depends only on start and end positions, not on the path taken, and can be associated with a stored potential energy. Non-conservative forces (e.g., kinetic friction, air drag) convert mechanical energy to heat, making the path matter. This umbrella sets up why potential energy is only definable for conservative forces.

in_depth.label: Identifying conservative forces
in_depth.description: Classify a force as conservative if the work it does on any round trip is zero (equivalently, if the work is path-independent and depends only on endpoints); examples include gravity and spring force. Contrast with non-conservative forces — friction, air resistance, applied external forces — whose work depends on path and dissipates mechanical energy. This focuses on the classification; potential energy storage is a separate umbrella.
examples: kinetic friction: longer path → more energy lost → non-conservative
spring force is conservative; $W_{spring}$ depends only on endpoints

in_depth.label: Potential energy as stored work of a conservative force
in_depth.description: Recognize that a potential energy function $U$ can be defined only for conservative forces, such that $W_{conservative} = -\Delta U$; doing negative work against a conservative force stores potential energy. This focuses on the conceptual link between conservative work and $U$; the specific forms $U = mgh$ and $U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ are developed in the next umbrella.
examples: releasing stored PE → conservative force does positive work
non-conservative forces cannot store potential energy

umbrellas.label: Kinetic Energy
umbrellas.description: The energy of motion of a translating object, given by $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$.

intro.label: Kinetic Energy Overview
intro.description: Kinetic energy is the energy an object possesses because of its translational motion: $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$, measured in joules. It scales with the square of speed and linearly with mass. Understanding KE is essential for applying the work–kinetic energy theorem and the conservation of mechanical energy.

in_depth.label: Kinetic energy formula and units
in_depth.description: Calculate translational kinetic energy as $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$, where $m$ is mass in kg and $v$ is speed in m/s, giving energy in joules (J = kg·m²/s²). Recognize that KE depends on speed squared, so a factor-of-2 speed change gives a factor-of-4 KE change. This focuses on computing KE; linking KE change to net work is the Work–Kinetic Energy Theorem (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Dependence of KE on mass and speed
in_depth.description: Predict how kinetic energy changes when mass or speed is scaled: KE is linear in mass (double mass → double KE) but quadratic in speed (double speed → quadruple KE), with implications for why stopping distance grows rapidly with vehicle speed. This focuses on qualitative and ratio reasoning, not numerical computation (handled in the KE formula keyword).
examples: heavier vs. faster: compare KE by ratios

umbrellas.label: Gravitational & Spring Potential Energy
umbrellas.description: The two main forms of mechanical potential energy tested on the MCAT: gravitational PE near Earth's surface and elastic PE stored in a spring.

intro.label: Potential Energy Forms Overview
intro.description: Potential energy is stored mechanical energy associated with position or configuration. For objects near Earth's surface, $PE_{grav} = mgh$ measures energy stored by lifting against gravity. For an ideal spring, $PE_{spring} = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ measures energy stored by compressing or stretching from equilibrium. Both forms can be fully converted to kinetic energy in a frictionless system.
examples: PE converted to KE as object falls or spring releases

in_depth.label: Gravitational potential energy: $PE = mgh$
in_depth.description: Calculate gravitational potential energy near Earth's surface as $PE = mgh$, where $m$ is mass, $g \approx 9.8\,\text{m/s}^2$, and $h$ is height above a chosen reference level; $PE$ increases when $h$ increases and the reference level is arbitrary. This focuses on the local (flat-Earth) approximation, not the universal $-GMm/r$ form (beyond typical MCAT depth).
examples: raise 2 kg by 5 m: $\Delta PE = 98$ J
higher reference can give negative PE (sign is relative)

in_depth.label: Spring (elastic) potential energy: $PE = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$
in_depth.description: Calculate the elastic potential energy stored in an ideal spring as $PE_{spring} = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$, where $k$ is the spring constant (N/m) and $x$ is the displacement from equilibrium; $PE_{spring}$ is always non-negative and is maximum at maximum compression or extension. This focuses on stored energy, not the spring force $F = -kx$ (Hooke's law, which belongs to P7 on oscillation dynamics per the boundary).
examples: compress by $x$ or stretch by $x$: same PE
stiffer spring (larger $k$) stores more PE for same $x$

in_depth.label: Choosing and using a reference level for PE
in_depth.description: Recognize that the gravitational PE reference level ($h = 0$) is chosen by the problem-solver and that only changes in PE ($\Delta PE = mg\Delta h$) are physically meaningful; choosing a convenient reference (e.g., the lowest point of motion) simplifies energy conservation calculations. This focuses on the conceptual use of reference levels, not on computing $\Delta PE$ for specific scenarios (done in adjacent keywords).
examples: set $h=0$ at ground, lowest point, or initial position
only $\Delta PE$ matters in energy equations
negative PE is fine: it just means below the reference

umbrellas.label: Conservation of Mechanical Energy
umbrellas.description: The principle that total mechanical energy (KE + PE) is constant in systems with only conservative forces, and how non-conservative forces modify this.

intro.label: Conservation of Energy Overview
intro.description: In a system where only conservative forces do work, total mechanical energy $E = KE + PE$ remains constant: energy converts between kinetic and potential forms but the sum never changes. When non-conservative forces (friction, air drag) act, they remove mechanical energy as heat, so $\Delta E_{mech} = W_{non-conservative}$. Mastering this principle allows solving a wide range of motion problems without tracking forces at every instant.
examples: friction present: $E_{mech\ final} = E_{mech\ initial} + W_{friction}$

in_depth.label: Conservation of mechanical energy (conservative forces only)
in_depth.description: Apply $KE_i + PE_i = KE_f + PE_f$ to find unknown speeds or heights in frictionless scenarios — a falling object, a pendulum swing, or a ball rolling off a ramp — by equating total mechanical energy at two points. This focuses on the lossless case; adding non-conservative forces is handled in a separate keyword.
examples: pendulum: max PE at top ↔ max KE at bottom
frictionless roller coaster: higher hill → slower at top

in_depth.label: Energy dissipation by non-conservative forces
in_depth.description: Apply the modified energy equation $KE_i + PE_i + W_{nc} = KE_f + PE_f$ (equivalently $W_{friction} = \Delta E_{mech}$, which is negative) to account for thermal energy lost to friction or drag; the magnitude of lost mechanical energy equals the work done by the non-conservative force. This focuses on how friction modifies the energy balance, not on defining conservative forces (a separate keyword).
examples: block slides down ramp with friction: $\Delta E_{mech} = -f_k d$

in_depth.label: Selecting energy states and applying conservation
in_depth.description: Identify the initial and final energy states (choosing reference levels, listing all KE and PE terms, assessing which forces are conservative), then write and solve the energy equation — a systematic strategy applicable to projectiles, springs, pendulums, and inclines. This focuses on the problem-solving workflow; the underlying formulas appear in adjacent keywords.
examples: choose reference level to eliminate one PE term
identify all non-conservative forces before applying conservation

in_depth.label: Energy conservation with a spring
in_depth.description: Apply conservation of mechanical energy to spring–mass systems: at maximum compression/extension all energy is $PE_{spring} = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$; at equilibrium (natural length) all energy is $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$; intermediate positions split between the two forms ($\frac{1}{2}kx^2 + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = E_{total}$). This focuses on the spring–mass energy exchange; the oscillation period/frequency of SHM → P7 (boundary).
examples: at equilibrium: all PE converts to KE
intermediate position: split between PE and KE

umbrellas.label: Power
umbrellas.description: The rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts.

intro.label: Power Overview
intro.description: Power is the rate at which work is done: $P = W/t = Fv\cos\theta$, measured in watts (W = J/s). High power means delivering energy quickly; the same total work done faster requires more power. Power is relevant to engines, muscles, and any situation where the time-rate of energy delivery matters.
examples: $P = Fv$ for constant force parallel to velocity

in_depth.label: Power: $P = W/t$ and units
in_depth.description: Define power as the rate of doing work, $P = W/t$, with SI unit watt (W = J/s = kg·m²/s³); recognize that average power equals total energy transferred divided by time. This focuses on the basic definition and unit; expressing power in terms of force and velocity is a separate keyword.
examples: lift 100 J of work in 2 s: $P = 50$ W
same work, half the time → double the power

in_depth.label: Instantaneous power: $P = Fv\cos\theta$
in_depth.description: Calculate instantaneous power as $P = Fv\cos\theta$ (or simply $P = Fv$ when force is parallel to velocity), useful when a constant force acts on a moving object and time is not directly given. This focuses on the force–velocity form; the basic $P = W/t$ definition is a separate keyword.
examples: car engine force $F$ at speed $v$: $P = Fv$
push at angle $\theta$ to motion: $P = Fv\cos\theta$
constant power → force must decrease as speed increases

in_depth.label: Power and mechanical efficiency
in_depth.description: Apply the concept of mechanical efficiency $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100\%$: because non-conservative forces dissipate energy, a real machine delivers less useful power than it consumes; efficiency is always less than 100% when friction or drag is present. This focuses on the efficiency ratio; the energy dissipation mechanism is covered in the conservation-of-energy umbrella.
examples: 80% efficient motor: 20% of input power lost to heat
higher efficiency → less energy wasted per unit time
